title: Spontaneity and Thermodynamic Favorability
subject: AP Chemistry
objectives: Explain why spontaneous does not mean fast — thermodynamics vs. kinetics
Predict how temperature changes affect the spontaneity of reactions in each ΔH/ΔS category
Describe how coupled reactions allow nonspontaneous processes to occur in biological systems

sections.heading: Spontaneous Does Not Mean Instantaneous
sections.content:
One of the most common misconceptions in chemistry is confusing spontaneity with speed. A spontaneous reaction is thermodynamically favorable — ΔG < 0 — but it says nothing about how fast the reaction occurs.

Diamond converting to graphite is spontaneous (ΔG < 0 under normal conditions) — but it takes billions of years. The rusting of iron is spontaneous but takes weeks or months. The combustion of gasoline is spontaneous but requires a spark to get started.

Thermodynamics tells you WHETHER a reaction can happen. Kinetics (reaction rates, activation energy) tells you HOW FAST it happens. A reaction can be thermodynamically favorable but kinetically so slow that it effectively never occurs without a catalyst or an energy input to overcome the activation energy barrier.

sections.heading: Temperature and Spontaneity
sections.content:
Consider the decomposition of calcium carbonate:
CaCO₃(s) → CaO(s) + CO₂(g)   ΔH = +178 kJ, ΔS = +161 J/K

This is case 4 (ΔH > 0, ΔS > 0): spontaneous only at high temperatures.

Find the crossover temperature:
ΔG = 0 when T = ΔH/ΔS = 178,000 J / 161 J/K = 1106 K ≈ 833°C

Below 833°C: ΔG > 0 — CaCO₃ is stable (limestone stays solid).
Above 833°C: ΔG < 0 — CaCO₃ decomposes (this is how lime kilns work).

This is not just a textbook exercise — the cement industry heats limestone above this temperature to produce lime (CaO), one of the most important industrial chemicals in the world.

sections.heading: Coupled Reactions in Biology
sections.content:
Many essential biological reactions are nonspontaneous on their own. Building proteins from amino acids, synthesizing DNA, and pumping ions against their concentration gradient all have ΔG > 0. How do cells drive these reactions?

By coupling them to a strongly spontaneous reaction. The hydrolysis of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) releases about −30.5 kJ/mol:

ATP + H₂O → ADP + Pᵢ, ΔG = −30.5 kJ/mol

When a nonspontaneous reaction (ΔG = +15 kJ/mol) is coupled with ATP hydrolysis (ΔG = −30.5 kJ/mol), the overall ΔG is −15.5 kJ/mol — spontaneous! The cell pays for the unfavorable reaction with the energy stored in ATP.

This is why ATP is called the 'energy currency' of the cell. Your body hydrolyzes roughly 40 kg of ATP per day — recycling it continuously — to power all the nonspontaneous processes that keep you alive.

Spontaneous (ΔG < 0) means thermodynamically favorable, NOT fast. Temperature determines spontaneity when ΔH and ΔS have the same sign. Cells drive nonspontaneous reactions by coupling them with ATP hydrolysis, making the overall ΔG negative.

sections.heading: ΔG, Equilibrium, and the Relationship Between Them
sections.content:
There is a direct mathematical relationship between ΔG° (standard free energy change) and the equilibrium constant K:

ΔG° = −RT ln K

where R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) and T is in Kelvin.

If ΔG° < 0: K > 1, meaning products are favored at equilibrium.
If ΔG° > 0: K < 1, meaning reactants are favored at equilibrium.
If ΔG° = 0: K = 1, meaning neither is favored.

This equation unifies thermodynamics and equilibrium — two topics that might seem separate but are actually two perspectives on the same underlying reality. A large negative ΔG° means the reaction strongly favors products, which is the same thing as saying K is very large.

Under nonstandard conditions, the actual ΔG depends on the reaction quotient Q:
ΔG = ΔG° + RT ln Q

When Q < K, ΔG < 0 and the reaction proceeds forward. When Q > K, ΔG > 0 and the reaction proceeds in reverse. When Q = K, ΔG = 0 and the system is at equilibrium.

aiContext: AP Chemistry. Use dimensional analysis for all calculations. Stress WHY reactions happen, not just equation balancing. Common confusion: entropy vs enthalpy direction, and applying Le Chatelier to specific perturbations.
